_note: Ernest v1 translation set — 10 items at B1, EN coach language, themed school / sports / friends per profile. Targets his active_categories (Tenses, Articles, Conditionals, Phrasal Verbs, Vocabulary, Relative Clauses) with weight on Articles uncountable nouns (his measured 0% weak pattern). Short prompts to stay gentle on his input-scaffolding gap (input accuracy lags gap-type by ~30 pts). English explanations with Russian-L1-source called out where the trap is calque-driven.

exercises.source_observation: 'information' is uncountable in English — never takes 'a/an', no plural -s. Targets Ernest's documented weak pattern (uncountable nouns at 0%). School theme.
correct_answers: I need information about the project
I need some information about the project
I need information on the project
I need information about the project.

common_errors.feedback: Russian L1 trap: 'информация' is countable in Russian, but English 'information' is UNCOUNTABLE — no 'a/an', no plural -s. ✓ 'I need information' / 'some information', ✗ 'an information' / 'informations'.
✗ 'informations' — 'information' has NO plural form in English. To count it, use 'pieces of information' or 'some information'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I need information about the project'. 'information' is uncountable in English — no a/an, no plural -s. Same applies to: advice, news, knowledge, furniture.

exercises.source_observation: 'advice' is uncountable in English. Russian 'совет' is countable — direct calque produces 'an advice'. Friends theme (giving advice).
correct_answers: He gave me good advice
He gave me some good advice
He gave me a piece of good advice
He gave me good advice.

common_errors.feedback: Russian L1 trap: 'совет' is countable, but English 'advice' is UNCOUNTABLE — no 'a/an'. ✓ 'good advice' / 'a piece of advice'. ✗ 'a good advice' / 'an advice'.
✗ 'advices' — 'advice' has no plural in English. To count: 'pieces of advice'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'He gave me good advice' (or 'a piece of good advice' to count one). 'advice' is uncountable — no 'a', no plural.

exercises.source_observation: 'progress' is uncountable. Common Ernest error — 'a good progress' was specifically called out in his coach_notes weak_patterns. School achievement theme.
exercises.prompt_ru: Мы достигли хорошего прогресса в школе.
correct_answers: We made good progress at school
We've made good progress at school
We have made good progress at school
We made a lot of progress at school

common_errors.feedback: Russian L1 trap: 'прогресс' is countable, but English 'progress' is UNCOUNTABLE. ✓ 'made good progress' / 'a lot of progress', ✗ 'a good progress'.
✗ 'do progress' — wrong verb. The collocation is 'MAKE progress' (not 'do'). ✓ 'made progress', 'making progress'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'We made good progress at school'. 'progress' is uncountable (no 'a'); the verb is 'MAKE progress' (not 'do').

exercises.source_observation: Present perfect for life experience (have been + count). Russian uses past simple ('был два раза'), so direct calque gives wrong tense. Bahrain-Dubai context.
exercises.prompt_ru: Я был в Дубае два раза.
correct_answers: I have been to Dubai twice
I've been to Dubai twice
I have visited Dubai twice
I've visited Dubai twice
I have been to Dubai two times

common_errors.pattern_label: I was in Dubai (past simple calque)
common_errors.feedback: Russian L1 trap: «был» = past simple in Russian, but for life experience (how many times in your life) English uses PRESENT PERFECT. ✓ 'I have been to Dubai twice'. (Past simple 'I was in Dubai' is fine if you mean a specific past time: 'I was in Dubai last year'.)

common_errors.pattern_label: have been IN Dubai (wrong prep for travel)
common_errors.feedback: For travel/visit experience, use 'been TO': ✓ 'have been TO Dubai'. 'Been in' suggests location at a moment, not a visit.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I have been to Dubai twice' (or 'I've been to Dubai twice'). Present perfect for life-experience count; preposition 'to' for places visited.

exercises.source_observation: Present continuous for action happening now. Russian 'играет' is ambiguous (simple or continuous); English requires BE + V-ing for 'right now'. Sports theme.
exercises.prompt_ru: Он играет в футбол прямо сейчас.
correct_answers: He is playing football right now
He's playing football right now
He's playing football at the moment
He is playing football right now.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'He plays right now' — present simple is for habits/repeats, not for 'right now'. For an action happening AT THIS MOMENT use present continuous: ✓ 'He is playing right now'.
Missing auxiliary 'is' — present continuous needs BE + V-ing: ✓ 'He IS playing' or 'He's playing'. Never just 'He playing'.
Russian L1 trap: 'играть В футбол' calques to 'play IN football'. English uses NO preposition: ✓ 'play football', ✗ 'play in football'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'He IS playing football right now'. 'right now' = present continuous (BE + V-ing); no preposition before 'football'.

exercises.source_observation: 1st conditional: if + present, will + base. Russian uses future in both clauses ('если пойдёт дождь, мы останемся'); English never uses 'will' in the if-clause.
exercises.prompt_ru: Если завтра пойдёт дождь, мы останемся дома.
correct_answers: If it rains tomorrow, we'll stay home
If it rains tomorrow, we will stay home
If it rains tomorrow, we'll stay at home
We'll stay home if it rains tomorrow
If it rains tomorrow we'll stay home

common_errors.feedback: Russian L1 trap: «если пойдёт» uses future, but English RULE: NEVER use 'will' after 'if' in conditionals. ✓ 'If it RAINS tomorrow' (present), ✗ 'If it will rain'. Will only goes in the main clause.
The main clause needs 'will' (or 'll) for a future result: ✓ 'we WILL stay' / 'we'll stay'. Without 'will' it sounds like a habit, not a future plan.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'If it RAINS tomorrow, we'll STAY home'. 1st conditional rule: if + PRESENT, WILL + base. Never put 'will' in the if-clause.

exercises.source_observation: 2nd conditional: if + past simple, would + base. Russian 'если бы у меня было' calques to 'if I would have' (would in if-clause).
exercises.prompt_ru: Если бы у меня было больше времени, я бы выучил испанский.
correct_answers: If I had more time, I would learn Spanish
If I had more time, I'd learn Spanish
I would learn Spanish if I had more time
I'd learn Spanish if I had more time
If I had more time I would learn Spanish

common_errors.feedback: Russian L1 trap: «если бы» feels like 'would' to Russians, but English RULE: NEVER use 'would' after 'if'. ✓ 'If I HAD more time' (past simple), ✗ 'If I would have'. 'Would' only goes in the main clause.

common_errors.pattern_label: if I have (present, wrong tense for hypothetical)
common_errors.feedback: For a hypothetical/imaginary situation use PAST SIMPLE in the if-clause: ✓ 'If I HAD more time' (= I don't have it now). 'If I have' would be a real possibility, but 'would' in main clause signals it's hypothetical — needs 'had'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'If I HAD more time, I WOULD learn Spanish'. 2nd conditional rule: if + PAST SIMPLE, WOULD + base. Never put 'would' in the if-clause.

exercises.source_observation: 'turn off' separable PV. School/home context. Tests both inseparable production ('turn off the lights') and separable variant ('turn the lights off').
correct_answers: Don't forget to turn off the lights
Don't forget to turn the lights off
Don't forget to switch off the lights
Don't forget to switch the lights off
Don't forget to turn the light off
Don't forget to turn off the light

common_errors.feedback: Russian L1 trap: «выключить» feels like 'close' to Russians (closing a switch), but English uses 'TURN OFF' or 'SWITCH OFF' for lights/devices. ✗ 'close the lights', ✓ 'turn off the lights'.
✗ 'off the lights' — 'off' alone is a preposition, not a verb. You need a verb in front: ✓ 'TURN off' or 'SWITCH off'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'Don't forget to TURN OFF the lights' (or 'turn the lights off' — both work because 'turn off' is separable).

exercises.source_observation: 'win' irregular past = 'won'. Common error 'winned'. Tests 'win the match' collocation (vs 'win at' / 'win in').
correct_answers: We won the match yesterday
We won the game yesterday
We won yesterday's match
We won the match yesterday.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'winned' doesn't exist. 'win' is irregular: win → WON → won. Past tense = 'won' (rhymes with 'one').
Russian L1 trap: 'выиграть В' or 'выиграть НА' calques to 'win at/in'. English uses NO preposition with the match itself: ✓ 'won the match'. ('Win at' is OK with skills: 'win at chess'.)

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'We WON the match yesterday'. 'win' → 'won' (irregular); no preposition before 'the match'.

exercises.source_observation: Subject relative clause with 'who' for people. Russian 'который' covers all relatives; English splits who/which/that. Friends theme.
exercises.prompt_ru: Мальчик, который сидит рядом со мной, мой друг.
correct_answers: The boy who sits next to me is my friend
The boy that sits next to me is my friend
The boy who sits beside me is my friend
The boy who sits next to me is my friend.

common_errors.feedback: Russian L1 trap: 'который' = both who/which in Russian, but English splits — use 'WHO' (or 'that') for PEOPLE, 'which' (or 'that') for THINGS. ✓ 'the boy WHO sits', ✗ 'the boy which sits'.
✗ 'the boy what sits' — 'what' is not a relative pronoun for people in standard English. Use 'WHO' (or 'that') for people: ✓ 'the boy who sits'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'The boy WHO sits next to me is my friend'. 'who' for people, 'which' for things, 'that' works for both.
